rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. The published Massachusetts rank of 2 is an artifact of that defect and must not be carried forward. Massachusetts must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
score_delta_note: The delta of -35 points measures the correction of a data defect, NOT institutional decline. The published 94.4 was a bulk-import placeholder carrying last_assessed: null and had no evidentiary basis whatsoever. This assessment is the first evidence-based baseline for Massachusetts. No conclusion about a change in Massachusetts institutional performance should be drawn from the delta.

band_change.note: Band change from exemplary to Functional reflects replacement of an unevidenced placeholder with an evidence-based baseline, not a decline in Massachusetts performance. See score_delta_note.

calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt. Massachusetts was published at rank 2 with composite 94.4 and last_assessed null: a bulk-import placeholder with no evidentiary basis. Calibrated instead to the methodology anchors and to the 17 states assessed at full depth: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Massachusetts at 59.4 sits between New York and Illinois, near the top of the assessed set but below New Jersey. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":59.4,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.8, EMP 3.2, ACT 3.6, EQU 3.6, BND 2.4, ACC 3.4, SYS 3.8, INT 3.2. baseComposite 59.375; stdDev 0.429 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 59.4, band Functional. BAND UNSTABLE: 59.4 is 0.6 below the 60.0 Functional/Established boundary. Four sensitivity cases were run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: BND 2.4 to 3.0 (shelter refusal and Bridgewater consent remediated) returns {"composite":61.2,"band":"Established","integrationPremium":0}; AB3 3 to 2 (Virginia rule rejected, transparency floored) returns {"composite":58.7,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; all dimensions minus 0.2 returns {"composite":54.4,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; all dimensions plus 0.2 returns {"composite":64.4,"band":"Established","integrationPremium":0}. A single 0.6-point improvement in BND alone moves Massachusetts into the Established band, so the band should be treated as provisional pending the next assessment cycle.

cross_state_consistency_flag.rule_under_test: AB3 Transparency: where a broad executive or legislative records exemption coexists with an independently funded body publishing damaging findings, cap AB3 at 3 rather than flooring at 2.
cross_state_consistency_flag.test_case: Massachusetts is the strongest available test. Its records-law exemptions are the broadest in the United States, while its State Auditor and Inspector General are unusually productive.
cross_state_consistency_flag.finding: RULE HOLDS, with one rider. Massachusetts is the only state where the governor, the Legislature and the judiciary all claim complete exemption from the public records law (Boston Globe, 2025-03-17), and the Boston Globe found loopholes, delays and poor enforcement across what remains (2026-03-04). On exemption breadth alone Massachusetts would floor at 2. But the independently funded bodies are not decorative. State Auditor Diana DiZoglio published an audit finding that hospital financial conditions were not appropriately monitored, including at Steward facilities. Inspector General Jeffrey Shapiro published a February 2026 report calling the sheriff departments budget process opaque, chaotic and deeply flawed, with a $110 million deficit against more than $725 million budgeted. Both are damaging findings about state conduct, published within the last 18 months, that no external party compelled. That output satisfies anchor 3, at least one report disclosing an unflattering finding, and exceeds anchor 2, failures disclosed only when legally required. AB3 is therefore scored 3, not 2.
cross_state_consistency_flag.rider: Massachusetts also surfaces a limit the Virginia formulation does not cover. The Legislature is actively obstructing the oversight body the rule depends on. Voters approved legislative audit authority by 72 percent in 2024, and the Auditor still had to sue legislative leaders in February 2026 to compel compliance. The rule should be adopted benchmark-wide with the rider that the cap of 3 applies only while the oversight body is still producing published findings. If obstruction succeeds in reducing that output to nothing, the floor of 2 becomes correct again. In Massachusetts the obstruction has not yet stopped output, so 3 stands.
cross_state_consistency_flag.recommendation: Adopt the rule benchmark-wide, amended with the output-contingency rider above.
cross_state_consistency_flag.scoring_effect: AB3 scored 3 rather than 2. This raises ACC from 3.2 to 3.4 and the composite from 58.7 to 59.4, a difference of 0.7 points, verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions on both dimension sets. It does not change the band; both values fall in Functional. The rule therefore matters for the score but is not load-bearing for the band.

attribution_note: Scores Massachusetts state conduct only, across all three branches, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. The window spans two governors, Baker through January 2023 and Healey from January 2023, and a Democratic supermajority Legislature throughout. Items are attributed to the Commonwealth regardless of branch: the Legislature refusal to comply with a voter-approved audit is scored as state conduct under AB3 and I4, as is the Healey administration curtailment of the right-to-shelter guarantee under B3, B4 and I1. Federal policy imposed on Massachusetts is excluded. The 2025 federal reconciliation law changes to Medicaid, which the state projects will cost roughly $3.5 billion, are not scored against the state; what is scored is state discretion in response, including the FY2027 proposed cuts to Adult Foster Care, Personal Care Attendant and Adult Day Health programs. Federal immigration enforcement is likewise not scored as state conduct; what is scored is the state discretionary response, including the protected-areas executive order, the sensitive-locations bill and the ICE misconduct reporting portal. The Steward Health Care bankruptcy was a corporate event; what is scored is the state regulatory failure the Auditor documented and the statutory correction that followed. Bridgewater State Hospital is operated by a private contractor, Recovery Solutions, under Department of Correction authority; conditions there are scored as state conduct because the state holds the custodial duty.
key_evidence: Massachusetts has the lowest uninsured rate in the United States at 2.1 percent, against a national rate of 8.2 percent, per the biennial Massachusetts Health Insurance Survey published December 2025.

Massachusetts is the only state in which the governor, the Legislature and the judiciary all claim complete exemption from the state public records law. Only 12 percent of state legislators told the Boston Globe in March 2025 that they should be covered by it.
Voters gave the State Auditor authority to audit the Legislature by 72 percent in 2024. Legislative leaders refused to comply, and Auditor Diana DiZoglio sued them in February 2026 to force it.

Massachusetts ended its 42-year right-to-shelter guarantee. In March 2026 the state planned to cut the family shelter system to about 3,200 units while lawmakers noted half the beds sat empty and some homeless families were still turned away.

Between July and December 2025, 528 families were forced out of Massachusetts shelters on reaching the six-month limit. Families who use overflow sites for five days are then barred from the waitlist for six months.

At Bridgewater State Hospital, staff used forced medication as restraint on 299 occasions between June and December 2025, a 62 percent rise over the prior six months, and the Disability Law Center found the circumstances did not always meet the legal standard.

The MBTA eliminated every subway slow zone by December 2024, replacing 248,000 feet of track, and the Federal Transit Administration gave the state oversight body a favorable review after previously citing major safety lapses.
The Fair Share Amendment raised more than $3 billion in the first ten months of fiscal 2026, funding universal free school meals and free community college.

Massachusetts paid $56 million to settle the Holyoke Soldiers Home COVID-19 outbreak that killed at least 76 veterans, with a minimum of $400,000 per deceased veteran estate, and then required both state veterans homes to obtain Department of Public Health licensure.

Black people in Massachusetts are imprisoned at 7.9 times the rate of white people. The state prison population fell nearly 50 percent in a decade, but racial disparities widened after the 2018 reforms.
